does: This paper presents a framework for multi-label structured prediction on domain-specific tasks by fine-tuning a small language model (LLaMA 3.1 8B) using LoRA with only 219 curated examples, combined with rule-based post-processing. Applied to compliance evaluation of conversational transcripts with 18 output fields, the system achieves 100% JSON structural validity and 83.0% overall accuracy on blind evaluation of 53 production transcripts. The approach incorporates hard-negative augmentation for critical decision boundaries and couples neural prediction with deterministic symbolic processing to ensure output validity. Inference runs in ~2 seconds on a single A100 GPU, reducing latency 2-5x compared to frontier LLM APIs and cutting costs to $0.013 per evaluation—46-76% cheaper than proprietary alternatives—while maintaining data privacy by avoiding external services.
connects: This work extends the line of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods pioneered by LoRA, applying these techniques to the practical problem of deploying specialized language models in resource-constrained settings. It builds on prior research combining neural models with rule-based systems for structured prediction, particularly in compliance and information extraction domains where output format validation is critical. The paper responds to known limitations of frontier LLMs—their prohibitive latency, cost, and privacy concerns in production deployments—by demonstrating that small models with targeted training data can match performance on domain-specific tasks. It incorporates data augmentation strategies for handling imbalanced or critical decision boundaries, connecting to work on few-shot learning and low-resource domain adaptation. The hybrid neural-symbolic approach echoes earlier efforts to ground language model outputs in logical constraints.
enables: This framework opens pathways for deploying specialized language models across regulated industries where cost, latency, and privacy are paramount—particularly in compliance, healthcare, and legal document analysis. The demonstrated success with minimal training data (219 examples) suggests broader applicability for domain-customization when labeled data is scarce. Future work could extend the hybrid decomposition to other structured prediction tasks beyond compliance evaluation, potentially formalizing design patterns for choosing which constraints to enforce symbolically versus learning neurally. The approach enables organizations to maintain internal models rather than rely on proprietary APIs, supporting research into how small models can substitute for frontier systems on well-defined domain tasks. It also points toward investigating optimal data augmentation strategies and whether similar cost-latency-accuracy tradeoffs apply across different domains and model sizes.
